skill.name: langchain-architecture
skill.description: Design LLM applications using the LangChain framework with agents, memory, and tool integration patterns. Use when building LangChain applications, implementing AI agents, or creating complex LLM workflows.
skill.summary: Master LangChain framework for building LLM applications with agents, chains, memory, and tool integration patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 27 static findings are false positives. The skill contains Python code examples in markdown documentation. The scanner incorrectly identified 'external_commands' (markdown code blocks), 'weak cryptographic algorithm' (agent type constants containing 'DESCRIPTION'), and 'network reconnaissance' (use case descriptions). This is educational documentation about LangChain with no executable code, network requests, or cryptographic operations.

low_findings.title: External Commands Detection (False Positive)
low_findings.description: Scanner detected 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' at 20 locations. These are Python code examples within markdown code blocks (e.g., `from langchain.agents import...`). No shell command execution exists.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Python code examples in markdown documentation. No shell execution present - the backticks are markdown code fences, not shell command operators.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Detection (False Positive)
low_findings.description: Scanner flagged 'weak cryptographic algorithm' at 6 locations. These are LangChain AgentType constants (CONVERSATIONAL_REACT_DESCRIPTION, ZERO_SHOT_REACT_DESCRIPTION) - not cryptographic algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Scanner incorrectly matches 'DESCRIPTION' as a crypto term. These are agent type enums in LangChain library - completely unrelated to cryptography.

low_findings.title: Network Reconnaissance Detection (False Positive)
low_findings.description: Scanner flagged 'network reconnaissance' at SKILL.md:24. The line describes 'Building autonomous AI agents with tool access' - a legitimate use case, not network reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: This is a standard LangChain use case description. No network scanning or reconnaissance code is present in this documentation skill.

content.value_statement: This skill helps developers design and implement production-grade LLM applications using LangChain framework patterns for agents, chains, memory, and tool integration.
actual_capabilities: Design agent architectures using ReAct, OpenAI Functions, and Structured Chat patterns
Implement conversation memory systems with buffer, summary, and vector store options
Build retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipelines with document loaders and vector stores
Create multi-step chains for complex LLM workflows with sequential and router chains
Add custom callbacks for logging, monitoring, and observability
Optimize performance with caching, batch processing, and streaming responses
limitations: Requires OpenAI API or compatible LLM provider access
Does not include actual API keys or authentication setup
Skill provides patterns and examples, not a deployable application
Does not cover newer LangChain v0.3+ API changes

use_cases.title: AI Application Developer
use_cases.description: Build applications that use LLM agents with tool access to automate multi-step workflows

use_cases.title: Data Engineer
use_cases.description: Create RAG pipelines that combine internal documents with LLM capabilities for intelligent querying
use_cases.target_user: Data engineers implementing document search and Q&A systems

use_cases.title: ML Engineer
use_cases.description: Design production-ready LLM systems with proper memory management, error handling, and observability
use_cases.target_user: Machine learning engineers deploying LLM applications to production

prompt_templates.title: Basic Agent Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a simple LangChain agent that can use a search tool and calculator. Show how to initialize the agent, add tools, and run a query.
prompt_templates.scenario: Setting up a basic conversational agent with tool access

prompt_templates.title: RAG Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to build a retrieval-augmented generation system using LangChain. Include document loading, text splitting, vector store creation, and the QA chain.

prompt_templates.title: Memory Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain the different memory types in LangChain and show code for implementing ConversationBufferMemory, ConversationSummaryMemory, and ConversationEntityMemory.

prompt_templates.title: Production Deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: What are the best practices for deploying LangChain applications to production? Include error handling, callbacks for monitoring, rate limiting, and testing strategies.
prompt_templates.scenario: Preparing a LangChain app for production

output_examples.input: How do I choose the right memory type for my LangChain agent?
output_examples.output: For short conversations under 10 messages, use ConversationBufferMemory to store all messages. For longer conversations, use ConversationSummaryMemory to summarize old messages. For tracking specific entities, use ConversationEntityMemory. For semantic retrieval of relevant history, use VectorStoreRetrieverMemory.

output_examples.input: Create a multi-step chain that extracts entities, analyzes them, and generates a summary.
output_examples.output: Use SequentialChain with three LLMChain components: extract_prompt extracts entities, analyze_prompt analyzes them, and summary_prompt generates the final summary. Set output_variables to capture all intermediate and final outputs.

best_practices: Always validate and sanitize user inputs before passing to LLM chains to prevent prompt injection
Implement proper error handling with try-catch blocks around agent and chain executions
Use callbacks for logging and monitoring token usage, latency, and errors in production
anti_patterns: Do not exceed context window limits - implement proper memory truncation or summarization
Avoid poorly defined tool descriptions - agents rely on descriptions to select the right tool
Do not skip input validation - validate all user inputs before using them in chains or prompts

faq.question: What is LangChain?
faq.answer: LangChain is a framework for building LLM applications. It provides abstractions for chains (sequences of calls), agents (systems that use LLMs to decide actions), memory (context storage), and tools (functions agents can call).

faq.question: Do I need an API key to use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes, this skill helps you design LangChain applications but you will need an OpenAI API key or another LLM provider key to actually run the code examples.

faq.question: What is the difference between an agent and a chain?
faq.answer: A chain is a predetermined sequence of steps. An agent uses an LLM to decide which actions to take dynamically, making it more flexible for complex tasks.

faq.question: How do I add memory to my agent?
faq.answer: Import the memory type you need (like ConversationBufferMemory), initialize it with memory_key, and pass it to the agent via the memory parameter when calling initialize_agent.

faq.question: What is RAG in LangChain?
faq.answer: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) combines document retrieval with LLM generation. Use document loaders, text splitters, vector stores, and RetrievalQA chains to build this.

faq.question: Can I use Claude or Codex with LangChain?
faq.answer: Yes, LangChain supports various LLM providers. You can use ChatAnthropic for Claude models or OpenAI-compatible interfaces for Codex.
